coredata.dc:title: Rational design of efficient near-infrared photon conversion channel via dual-upconversion process for superior photocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuanyuan
Lan, Xuefang
Park, Sung Heum
Wang, Lili
Liu, Dandan
Shi, Jinsheng
coredata.dc:description: Utilization of up-conversion materials is an effective strategy to realize near-infrared (NIR) light driven photocatalysis. However, their wide emission range lead to the low utilization efficiency of near-infrared light. In this paper, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and NaYF4: Yb3+, Tm3+ (NYF) are used to construct efficient photon conversion channel, which could realize effective utilization of NIR light via dual-upconversion process. Under 980 nm laser light irradiation, NYF emits discontinuous light in the range of 292–805 nm. Only lights below 519.8 nm can be absorbed by Ag3PO4 directly. The lights above 519.8 nm are also utilized by Ag3PO4 after the secondary conversion of CQDs. Such secondary light conversion significantly promotes the NIR light utilization efficiency by Ag3PO4. This work provides a new insight into designing and constructing superior photocatalyst by dual photon conversion channel for NIR light utilization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photon conversion channel
Dual-upconversion
CQDs
NYF
Photocatalyst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and verification of efficient photon conversion channel
Morphology, microstructure and element distribution analysis
Determination of CQDs contents in composites

National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The function mechanism of dual-UC process in NYF@C@Ag3PO4. Schematic illustration of synthetic process of (b) NYF@C@Ag3PO4 and (c) NYF@Ag3PO4@C composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UC emission spectrum of NYF under 980 nm laser light excitation. (b) UC emission spectra of CQDs under different excitation wavelengths. (c) ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectrum of Ag3PO4. (d) UC emission spectra of as-prepared samples under 980 nm laser light excitation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM and HR-TEM images of (a)–(c) NYF@C@Ag3PO4, (d) and (e) NYF @Ag3PO4@C. (f) SEM image of NYF@Ag3PO4@C and (f) conrresponding C distribution mapping. (h) SEM image of NYF@C@Ag3PO4 and (i) conrresponding C distribution mapping. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) CIP photodegradations of different samples under 980 nm laser light irradiation. (b) Apparent rate constants of different samples for CIP photodegradation. (c) CIP photodegradations of pure Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4@CQDs under visible light (>420 nm). (d) Apparent rate constants of NYF@C@Ag3PO4 composites with different contents for CIP photodegradations. (e) Apparent rate constants of different samples for photodegradation of other pollutants. (f) Cycling tests of NYF@C@Ag3PO4 for CIP photodegradation under 980 nm laser light irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Effect of CIP concentrations on photodegradation efficiency under 980 nm laser light irradiation and (b) corresponding apparent rate constants. (c) Effect of catalysts dosage on CIP photodegradation under 980 nm laser light irradiation and (d) corresponding apparent rate constants. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Plausible degradation pathway for CIP over NYF@C@Ag3PO4 composite under 980 nm laser light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Rational design of efficient near-infrared photon conversion channel via dual-upconversion process for superior photocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science, Qingdao Agricultural University, Chengyang District, Qingdao, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science, Qingdao Agricultural University, Chengyang District, Qingdao, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Pukyong National University, Busan, 608-737, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Pukyong National University, Busan, 608-737, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Utilization of up-conversion materials is an effective strategy to realize near-infrared (NIR) light driven photocatalysis. However, their wide emission range lead to the low utilization efficiency of near-infrared light. In this paper, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and NaYF4: Yb3+, Tm3+ (NYF) are used to construct efficient photon conversion channel, which could realize effective utilization of NIR light via dual-upconversion process. Under 980 nm laser light irradiation, NYF emits discontinuous light in the range of 292–805 nm. Only lights below 519.8 nm can be absorbed by Ag3PO4 directly. The lights above 519.8 nm are also utilized by Ag3PO4 after the secondary conversion of CQDs. Such secondary light conversion significantly promotes the NIR light utilization efficiency by Ag3PO4. This work provides a new insight into designing and constructing superior photocatalyst by dual photon conversion channel for NIR light utilization.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar light driven photocatalysis is a promising strategy to address imminent energy crisis and growing pollution issues owing to its low cost, high-efficiency and environmental friendliness [1–5]. However, realization of efficient photocatalytic activity is severely constrained by the poor utilization efficiency of NIR light irradiation, which accounts for 52% of the energy in solar spectrum [6–8]. This bottleneck is resulted from the mismatch between the wide bandgaps of materials and the low single NIR photon energy [9–11].

To address this issue, hybridization catalysts with lanthanide-doped materials is considered as one of the most promising approach as such materials can transform low-energy NIR excitation radiation into high-energy ultraviolet (UV) and visible emissions, allowing the re-absorbance and re-utilization by adjacent UV or visible-responsive components [12–14]. Nevertheless, the amounts of available photons are still low as the lanthanide-doped upconversion (UC) materials can only convert NIR light into widespread and discontinuous lights that are difficult to be used. For example, Wang et al. [15] built UC nanoparticles decorated ZnxCd1-xS to broaden light response range to NIR light region. While the converted light only wavelength below 529 nm can be utilized by ZnxCd1-xS and remnant lights are wasted, leading to a low NIR light utilization efficiency and a non-ideal photocatalytic activity.

CQDs is also regarded as an excellent UC materials, which could transform low-energy visible light to high-energy visible or UV light [16–18]. Unlike lanthanide-doped UC materials, light response of CQDs is not constrained to a specific excitation wavelength owing to their wide cross-section of UC absorption and a large range of emission spectra that promote energy band-gap matching [19–21]. This characteristic allows CQDs to reuse the emission peaks of UC materials and largely enhance UC efficiency of NIR irradiation [22–24]. Therefore, rational integration of rare earth-doped UC materials and CQDs is expected to contribute to a dual-component UC material, in which the exertion of their respective functions enables high NIR light capturing capability for photocatalysis.

Herein, for the first time, efficient photon conversion channel is constructed by dual UC process of rare earth doped UC material (NaYF4: Yb3+, Tm3+, marked as NYF) and CQDs. Under 980 nm laser light irradiation, NYF could emit discontinuous light in the range of 292–805 nm. Because light absorption edge of Ag3PO4 locates at 519.8 nm (determined in Fig. 2c), only light below 519.8 nm can be absorbed and utilized by Ag3PO4, leading to an inefficient utilization of emission band of NYF. As the emission spectrum of CQDs overlap with that of NYF, the residual light (>519.8 nm) from NYF is re-converted by CQDs to high-energy light (<519.8 nm), which largely improves the NIR light utilization efficiency of Ag3PO4 catalyst (Fig. 1 a). NaYF4: Yb3+, Tm3+@CQDs@Ag3PO4 composite, denoted NYF@C@Ag3PO4, is prepared by a stepwise strategy (Fig. 1b). NYF@Ag3PO4@CQDs, denoted NYF@Ag3PO4@C, is also prepared to investigate the influence of CQD location on photon conversion channel and photocatalytic activity (Fig. 1c). The detailed description is shown in experiment section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and verification of efficient photon conversion channel
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm the feasibility of dual photon conversion channel, UC emission spectra of NYF and CQDs, as well as ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) diffuse reflectance spectrum of Ag3PO4 are studied. As shown in Fig. 2a, under excitation of 980 nm laser light, emission peaks of NYF locate at both UV regions (292, 347, and 363 nm), visible regions (454, 476, 580, 648, and 692 nm) and NIR region (805 nm), which are attributed to the radioactive transitions of 1I6 → 3H6, 1I6 → 3F4, 1D2 → 3H6, 1D2 → 3F4, 1G4 → 3H6, 1D2 → 3H4, 1G4 → 3F4, 3F3 → 3H6, 3H4 → 3H6, respectively [25,26]. As for CQDs, excitation wavelengths of 580, 648, 692 and 805 nm are selected for spectrum characterization as they can be emitted from NYF but cannot be absorbed by Ag3PO4. Once excited by these lights, CQDs give rise to clearly UC emission bands in the range of 370–519.8 nm (Fig. 2b). As shown in Fig. 2c, as the light absorption edge of Ag3PO4 locates at 519.8 nm, only the emission peaks of NYF at 292, 347, 363, 454 and 476 nm could be utilized by Ag3PO4. The other emission peaks of NYF (580, 648, 692 and 805 nm) can also be utilized by Ag3PO4 after the re-conversion of CQDs, which transforms these lights into high-energy light (<519.8 nm). As can be seen from Fig. 2d, pure NYF presents strong UC emission peaks. However, a significant decrease can be observed when CQDs are decorated onto NYF, which further reduces even disappearances after the modification of Ag3PO4. According to previous reports [27–29], the coated CQDs and Ag3PO4 layers on the surface of NYF may block its light absorption ability (called light-shielding effect). In order to exclude such shielding effect, UC emission spectra of NYF are measured under different excitation powers. As shown in Fig. S2, all emission peaks simultaneously decrease as the excitation powers decrease, which is different from the peak change after decoration of CQDs or/and Ag3PO4 onto NYF (Fig. 2d). Therefore, the change of emission intensities from NYF to NYF@C@Ag3PO4 is not caused by faded excitation power density shielded by CQDs and Ag3PO4, but related to the re-absorption by them. These results suggest that an effective photon conversion channel is constructed and efficient energy transfer could be achieved from NYF and CQDs to Ag3PO4 [25]. Ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectrum is another effective tool to investigate the construction situation of photon conversion channel (Fig. S3). Compared with other samples, NYF@C@Ag3PO4 presents enhanced light absorption in the visible region, indicating effective absorption and utilization of visible light.

Fig. S5 presents the XRD parttens of as-prepared NYF@C@Ag3PO4 composite. Diffraction peaks of NYF are very sharp and strong, indicating the high crystallinity of UC material after calcination treatment [30,31], which is consistent with the results of SEM. All diffraction peaks of NYF match well with hexagonal NaYF4 (JCPDS 16–0334) with main peaks at 2θ of 17.20°, 30.06°, 30.78°, 43.49°, 53.28° and 53.75° indexed to the (1 0 0), (1 1 0), (1 0 1), (2 0 1), (3 0 0) and (2 1 1) planes of hexagonal NaYF4, respectively [32]. Besides, obvious diffraction peaks at 33.3° and 36.7° are observed in NYF@C@Ag3PO4, which are attributed to the (2 1 0) and (2 1 1) planes of cubic Ag3PO4 (JCPDS 74–1876) [33], confirming the successful decoration of Ag3PO4 on composite. In terms of CQDs, no obvious diffraction peaks were observed after decoration, which may be ascribed to intrinsic low crystallinity of CQDs [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, microstructure and element distribution analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Morphology and microstructure of samples are studied by SEM and TEM analysis. As shown in Fig. S6a, NYF presents hexagonal micro-rods with lengths about 6–8 μm and diameters about 1–2 μm. The TEM image (Fig. S7a) confirms the same catalyst structure as revealed by SEM. HR-TEM image confirms the presence of NYF crystalline with the lattice spacing of 0.297 nm assigning to the (1 1 0) plane of NYF (Fig. S7b). Surface of NYF is rather smooth, while many nanoparticles in the surface of NYF could be clearly seen after loading Ag3PO4, implying the successful fabrication of NYF@Ag3PO4 (Fig. S6c). TEM images in Figs. S7c and S7d further confirm the existence of Ag3PO4 via the presence of 0.268 nm lattice spacing ascribing to (2 1 0) plane of Ag3PO4. NYF@CQDs shows the similar morphology as NYF except its rough surface (Fig. S6d). The existence of CQD can be determined by HR-TEM with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm attributed to (1 0 0) plane of CQD (Fig. S7f). In comparison with NYF, the morphologies of NYF@Ag3PO4@C and NYF@C@Ag3PO4 still maintain perfect micro-rods (Figs. S6e and f). Both Ag3PO4 and CQDs could be observed on the surface of NYF@Ag3PO4@C and NYF@C@Ag3PO4, suggesting the successful fabrication of ternary composites (Fig. 3 a–e). Element mappings are provided to determine the distributions of C element in two composites (Fig. 3f–i). The other elements attribution of two composites are presented in Figs. S8 and S9, which exhibits a homogeneous distribution of Na, Y and F, Ag and P elements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CQDs contents in composites
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs as one of the UC material play a crucial role in the construction of photon conversion channel as well as the photocatalytic performance. To rule out such influence on NYF@Ag3PO4@C and NYF@C@Ag3PO4, C element contents investigation on both samples are conducted. Fig. S10 presents energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) spectra of two the composites and corresponding element contents of NYF@Ag3PO4@C and NYF@C@Ag3PO4 are presented in Tables S1 and S2, respectively, whose C contents are determined to be 12.88% and 12.66%, respectively (Fig. S11), suggest that the CQDs content would not be a factor influence catalytic performances of NYF@Ag3PO4@C and NYF@C@Ag3PO4.

Photocatalytic activity of samples was studied by choosing CIP (10 mg/L) as the model organic pollutant under 980 nm laser light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4 a, without Ag3PO4, NYF and NYF@C present negligible CIP photodagradation, suggesting Ag3PO4 the crucial role in photocatalyst. Besides, Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4@C also show no photocatalytic activity with the absence of NYF, which provides high-energy light (<519.8 nm) for Ag3PO4 utilization from the UC of 980 laser light. However, once NYF and Ag3PO4 combined, NYF@Ag3PO4 presents enhanced photocatalytic degradation and 28.67% of CIP is eliminated within 18 h. When dual photon conversion channel is constructed via NYF and CQDs, photocatalytic activity of NYF@C@Ag3PO4 is further improved. The CIP degradation efficiency of NYF@C@Ag3PO4 is 69.64% within 18 h, while only 47.45% of CIP is degraded for NYF@Ag3PO4@C. The CIP degradation kinetic constants of NYF@C@Ag3PO4 composite are calculated to be 0.065 h−1, which is about 1.8 folds than that (0.035 h−1) of NYF@Ag3PO4@C, and 3.3 folds than that (0.019 h−1) of NYF@Ag3PO4 (Fig. 4b). In order to verify the effect of CQDs layer on photo conversion channel, photocatalytic activities of pure Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4@CQDs (Fig. S13) are studied under visible light. As can be seen from Fig. 4c, Ag3PO4@CQDs exhibits enhanced photodegradation ability in comparison with pure Ag3PO4. These results suggest that the existence and location of CQDs layer play an important role for efficient photon channel, further influence photocatalytic activity. NYF@C@Ag3PO4 with different C element contents are also synthesized to optimize photocatalytic performance of composite (Figs. S14–S16). In Fig. 4d, photocatalytic activity of NYF@C@Ag3PO4 increases as the carbon source increases. When the radio of NYF and glucose is 1:3.6, NYF@C@Ag3PO4 shows the best CIP photodegradation rate. However, further increase of CQDs loading results in decreased degradation rates. This might be attributed to the CQDs inner filter effect via competition for the absorption of photons with Ag3PO4, thereby reducing the formation of reactive species [35].

The effect of initial CIP concentration (5–20 mg/L) on photodegradation performance of NYF@C@Ag3PO4 is shown in Fig. 5 a and corresponding kinetic constants are provided in Fig. 5b. CIP elimination efficiency decrease with its increased initial concentration, implying that NYF@C@Ag3PO4 composite is suitable for the treatment of low-concentration organic contaminants. When initial concentration is too high, intermediate products would accumulate on catalyst surface and block the active sites, leading to weak adsorption of CIP. The influence of catalyst dosages (from 0 to 1.5 g/L) on CIP degradation is also investigated. As shown in Fig. 5c and d, CIP degradation rate gradually improves with the increase of catalyst dosage. However, when sample dosage increase to 1.5 from 1 g/L, CIP degradation rate improves a little. This is because overdose of catalyst could lead to its aggregation, which could give rise to light scattering hence low light intensity. Fig. 4e shows photodegradation curves of other organic pollutants including RhB, MO and MB. NYF@C@Ag3PO4 exhibits enhanced photocatalytic performance in many other contaminants degradation. Cycling tests of NYF@C@Ag3PO4 for CIP degradation is used to evaluate its stability. As shown in Fig. 4f, CIP photodegradation efficiencies of NYF@C@Ag3PO4 change little after five cycles, suggesting its high stability.

Degradation intermediates of CIP are investigated by LC-MS/MS (Fig. S17) and possible intermediates are shown in Table S6. According to the results and previous papers [36], a plausible CIP photodegradation pathway is reasonably proposed in Fig. 6 . This pathway is the destruction of the piperazinyl moiety. Firstly, piperazine is opened and formed CIP 1. Subsequently, CIP 1 eliminates two –CO groups and change to CIP 4. The decomposition of CIP 4 is accompanied by the generation of CIP 5 via hydroxylation and losing CH–NH2. CIP 6 and CIP 7 could be achieved by the decarboxylation and decarbonylation of CIP 5, respectively. Finally, CIP 6 and CIP 7 translate to CIP 8 by decarbonylation and decarboxylation, respectively.

In summary, efficient photon conversion channel is constructed by dual UC process of CQDs and NYF for the first time, which significantly improves the light absorption of Ag3PO4. NYF could convert the 980 nm laser light to discontinuous light in the range of 292–805 nm. Nevertheless, only light below 519.8 nm can be directly absorbed and utilized by Ag3PO4, light above 519.8 nm can also be utilized after the second conversion via CQDs, which transform the low-energy light (>519.8 nm) into high-energy light (<519.8 nm). Such efficient photo conversion channel constructed by dual-upconversion material of NYF and CQDs, largely improving the utilization efficiency of NIR light by Ag3PO4 catalyst. Under 980 nm laser light irradiation, NYF, Ag3PO4, NYF@C and Ag3PO4@C samples have no photocatalytic activity, while NYF@C@Ag3PO4 composite exhibits obviously enhanced CIP photodegradation efficiency, which is more than 1.5 times higher than that of NYF@Ag3PO4@C, and 2.5 times higher than that of NYF@Ag3PO4. This work provides a new insight into designing and constructing efficient photocatalyst by dual photon conversion channel for NIR light utilization.

Yuanyuan Zhang: conceived of the presented idea, carried out the experiment. Xuefang Lan: Writing - original draft, wrote and revised the manuscript. Sung Heum Park: developed the theory and performed the computations. Lili Wang: developed the theory and performed the computations. Dandan Liu: carried out the experiment. Jinsheng Shi: conceived of the presented idea.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11804180) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017BEM013).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2020.07.065.
